classes.label: adverse event
classes.description: unintended event that has negative impact upon a care process

notes.text: 'Iatrogenesis' or 'iatrogeny' is a common cause of adverse events.
Adverse events can occur during appropriate care activities
Adverse events may cause harm

examples.text: An infection acquired during a hospital stay
A drug may be used as recommended but cause allergic reaction when the drug allergy was not known before the treatment.
An accident in connection to a care activity
A mishap during correctly performed care activity

classes.label: adverse event management
classes.description: set of care activities performed in response to an adverse event

classes.label: authorization by law
classes.description: provision in legislation that in certain circumstances may overrule the need for informed consent

notes.text: A care mandate requires either informed consent or authorization by law.

classes.label: automated care
classes.description: care initiated by a care actor and thereafter delivered by an automatic medical device

notes.text: Automated care is not a care activity in its own right since the automatic medical device doesn't have the capacity to be responsible. It is the care actor who initiates and reviews the automated care that is responsible for safe use of the automatic medical device.

examples.text: Activities performed by the machine during a long term Electrocardiography (“Holter recording”) programme, implanted cardiac defibrillator

classes.label: automatic medical device
classes.description: medical device capable of performing care activities by itself or under human supervision

examples.text: An electrocardiography machine
Electronic blood-sugar monitor

classes.label: care activity
classes.description: activity intended to improve or maintain a health state

notes.text: A care activity maintaining health state includes activities confirming a health state, e.g. a medical statement to an insurance company or the driving licence authority
Within this document, a care activity is always part of a care process; care activities outside of a care process, such as personal first aid, are not addressed by this document

examples.text: A blood pressure measurement completed by a care professional including component care activities of taking, documenting and evaluation

classes.label: care activity bundle
classes.description: set of care activities

notes.text: A care activities bundle may be delineated using a health thread comprising care activities.
A care activities bundle can be offered as a care service

examples.text: All care activities provided to a subject of care in provision of, during and in the aftermath of a planned surgical operation, in relation to that operation.

classes.label: care activity delay
classes.description: health related period during which a care activity is planned but not started

classes.label: care activity management
classes.description: care activity during which the status of care activities in a care plan are changed

notes.text: Example statuses for care activities are: planned, scheduled, resource allocated, ongoing, performed/finished, evaluated.

classes.label: care activity mandate
classes.description: care mandate assigning the right and obligation to perform specific care activities

classes.label: care activity period
classes.description: period during which care activities are performed for a subject of care

classes.label: care actor
classes.description: organization or person participating in care

notes.text: The involvement of the care actor can either be direct (for example, the actual provision of care) or indirect (for example, at organizational level).

According to this definition, people or organizations responsible for the funding, payment, or reimbursement of care provision are care actors, as are people and organizations responsible for care delivery.  If a person or organization only funds care and does not deliver care, it is a care actor but not a care provider.
N/A

classes.label: care appointment
classes.description: appointment for a contact

classes.label: care assessment
classes.description: care activity where an opinion is formed about health conditions of a subject of care

classes.label: care commitment
classes.description: acceptance of a care request including commitment by the care actor to perform requested care activities

notes.text: The care commitment is the promise by the care actor to perform care activities. This also means that the care provider accepts and confirms the pending care mandate issued through the proposed care plan.  It is only once the care commitment has been stated that an effective care mandate exists and will be the legal framework for all care activities of the subsequent care process.
Implicitly, a care commitment results from a dialogue with the subject of care or someone on behalf of the subject of care within a care needs assessment

classes.label: care communication
classes.description: care activity where at least two care actors communicate

classes.label: care contact
classes.description: interaction between one or more subject of care and one or more care personnel

examples.text: Face to face contact with a general practitioner
Telephone contact
Telemedicine contact
Family visit at the social worker’s office

classes.label: care data
classes.description: data produced during care activities

notes.text: N/A

classes.label: care documenting
classes.description: care activity where health records are created or maintained

classes.label: care employment
classes.description: mandate given by a care organization to a care personnel describing the roles and responsibilities assigned to that care personnel

classes.label: care evaluation
classes.description: care activity where aspects of at least one other care activity are evaluated

notes.text: Care evaluation may be performed by all kinds of care actors, including the subject of care.

classes.label: care funds
classes.description: resource provided for funding care delivery

notes.text: Funds may be provided by, for example, a health insurance fund, a governmental agency, a national or local authority, a welfare programme, the subject of care or any other person or organization having a role in the funding of care.

classes.label: care goal
classes.description: desired achievement of one or more care activities

notes.text: A set of care goals may constitute a health objective
A care goal shall be expressed as one or several target conditions to be reached within a specified date and time.

examples.text: To increase the subject of care's adherence to the treatment in a hypertension programme.
To stabilise the subject of care’s systolic and diastolic pressure in a hypertension programme.
To increase the subject of care’s knowledge of their disease through an educational programme for diabetics.

classes.label: care guideline
classes.description: set of systematically developed statements based on knowledge to assist the decisions made by care actors about care activities to be performed with regard to specified health issues

notes.text: Care guidelines are a knowledge resource.  They are generic and concern no actual subject of care in particular. They generally reflect a broad statement of good practice and may include multiple operational details.
Care guidelines are ordinarily structured and contain standard criteria and indicators for measurement.

examples.text: Care guidelines related to hypertension, diabetes, pregnancy follow-up, Caesarean section, pressure area management
Exercise programmes
Social intervention programmes for people with a learning disability

classes.label: care information
classes.description: information about a person relevant to their care

classes.label: care information for import
classes.description: care information that is a candidate for import into a professional health record after a care professional has confirmed its clinical relevance to that professional health record

classes.label: care information request
classes.description: request sent out by a care actor to another care actor for specific care information needed for the provision of care to a subject of care

notes.text: In order to fulfil the request, a mandate to export personal information is needed.
In EN 13940-1:2007 specific clinical information request was the preferred term for this concept.

classes.label: care investigation
classes.description: care activity with the intention to clarify one or more health conditions of a subject of care

notes.text: Care investigations add and improve information about aspects of a health state.

Some primary care investigations may have an intended or secondary therapeutic effect, while some activities can be both investigative and therapeutic in nature (e.g. fibroscopy, interventional cardiology).

classes.label: care mandate
classes.description: mandate based on a commitment and either an informed consent or an authorization by law defining the rights and obligations of one care actor with regard to their involvement in care processes performed for a specific subject of care

notes.text: A care mandate can be explicit or implicit

Relevant information related to care mandates (including demands for care, informed consents, objections, care commitments, etc.) is recorded in health records where the information is made available for concerned care actors as health concerns.
Typically a care mandate is assigned by one care actor to another.

classes.label: care needs assessment
classes.description: care assessment during which a care professional considers a subject of care’s health needs and determines needed care activities

notes.text: A care needs assessment precedes care planning.
Care needs assessments should be performed in a dialogue with the subject of care. The responsibility for a care needs assessment is held by a care professional.
The subjects of care interact with care professionals in care needs assessments and also describe their opinions on which care activities should be asked for in a request for care.

classes.label: care organization
classes.description: organization being a care provider

notes.text: Groupings or subdivisions of an organization, such as departments or sub-departments, may also be considered as organizations where there is need to identify them. The internal structure of an organization is described by its organizational pattern.  Therefore, an organization may be considered in itself as a standalone organization or as a superstructure containing departments and sub-departments, for instance, other lower level organizations. A care organization represents the role any such organization plays when it is involved in the direct provision of care activities.

examples.text: A care team
A group practice or a self-employed general practitioner
A hospital, a hospital department, or a hospital care unit
A social department or a municipality providing care (including social care)
A non-governmental organisation or a voluntary association providing care (including social care)

classes.label: care pathway
classes.description: detailed guidance for each stage in the interdisciplinary management of a subject of care

notes.text: The concept care pathway includes subtypes, for example, 'integrated care pathways', 'multidisciplinary pathways of care', 'collaborative care pathways'.
Care pathways are designed to support care administration and care resource management. They provide detailed guidance for each stage in the management of a patient (treatments, interventions, etc.).
Care pathways are knowledge supporting the design of core care plans

classes.label: care performer
classes.description: role adopted by care personnel when performing care

classes.label: care period mandate
classes.description: care mandate commissioning a mandated period of care

notes.text: A care period mandate may be an agreement between the subject of care and a care provider to provide specified care services in a mandated period of care.

classes.label: care personnel
classes.description: person holding a care employment from a care organization

examples.text: nurse’s assistant, head of department, medical secretary, receptionist

classes.label: care plan
classes.description: dynamic, personalized plan including identified needed care activity, health objectives and care goals, relating to one or more specified health needs in a care process

notes.text: A care plan can be subdivided from different perspectives by different constraints.

examples.text: Integrated care plan
Plan to address one vulnerability
Plan for a single contact
Plan for social care activities to be performed by one care provider within a social care context
A social worker care plan with the constraint of only one specific social care provider involved

classes.label: care planning
classes.description: care activity management where a care plan is managed

classes.label: care process
classes.description: set of interrelated or interacting care activities which transforms inputs into outputs

notes.text: A care process is not by definition restricted to one care provider or any other organizational unit borders.

classes.label: care process evaluation
classes.description: care evaluation where a care process is evaluated against requirements

classes.label: care professional
classes.description: person holding a care professional entitlement

notes.text: The care professional entitlement entitles a care professional to provide care independent of a role in a care organization.

examples.text: General practitioner, medical consultant, therapist, dentist, nurse, radiographer,  social worker etc.

classes.label: care professional entitlement
classes.description: professional care mandate held by a person concerning specific roles in care

notes.text: Entitlement is usually backed by evidence of having received, or continuously receiving, the necessary qualification, the relevant education and training.
The official entitlement of a care professional forms the foundation for his/her official duties and rights.

examples.text: Diploma, professional registration (e.g. registered nurse)

classes.label: care provider
classes.description: care actor that is able to be assigned one or more care period mandates

notes.text: The personnel of a care organization that is a care provider may be care professionals and other care personnel who participate in the provision of care.

This International Standard includes only two specializations of care provider. This is not meant to exclude the possibility of other specializations. In jurisdictions where other kinds of care actors are included in the concept of care provider, the necessary specializations may be added.

According to this definition, organizations solely responsible for the funding, payment, or reimbursement of care provision are not care providers; for the purpose of this International Standard they are considered as care third parties.

classes.label: care provider activity
classes.description: care activity performed by a care provider

notes.text: A care provider activity can be performed in relation to several care activity period elements of the same care activity period.

classes.label: care resource
classes.description: resource needed to perform care activities

notes.text: As a care process develops, the care resources typically follow a life cycle. Examples of steps of such a life cycle are: 'available', 'booked', 'provided', 'in use', 'consumed', etc.

examples.text: Care professional on duty
Operation theatre
Instruments ready to use
Consultation rooms
Bed in a ward
Prepared medicinal products
Electrocardiography-device
Blood sample
Donated kidney

classes.label: care service
classes.description: result of a care process

classes.label: care service directory
classes.description: directory of the types of care services offered by one or more care providers

classes.label: care service offering
classes.description: set of care activities offered by care provider

notes.text: A process consists of activities. These may or may notcan be bundled in groups called services.
A service can be an entire process or a part of a process. In the second case, the result of the service contributes to the outcome of the process.

examples.text: Diagnostic investigation and result report.

classes.label: care supporting organization
classes.description: organization that is a care third party

examples.text: voluntary aid organization, homecare service organization, health insurance fund, family
health technology provider

classes.label: care third party
classes.description: care actor other than a care provider or the subject of care

notes.text: Care third party is an abstract concept.  It is fully supported through the use of one of the specific subordinate parties

examples.text: Funder of care

classes.label: care third party activity
classes.description: care activity performed by a care third party

examples.text: The care treatment of a bedsore by a subject of care's relation
care treatment on advice by a chemist

classes.label: care treatment
classes.description: care activity intended to directly improve or maintain a health state

notes.text: The treatment of a subject of care's health state, based on that subject of care's health conditions, is a part of the continuity of care process considered from a therapeutic perspective.
Care treatment is intended to contribute to fulfilling the assessed health need.
Prevention is a type of care treatment where the risk of a health condition is treated.
Care treatment includes all types of activities that intend to influence the health state including, for example, rehabilitation, palliative care, education of subjects of care and carers, etc.

classes.label: clinical care
classes.description: care activity concerned with physical or mental health needs

classes.label: clinical care activity mandate
classes.description: care mandate to provide needed care activities concerned with the physical or mental health of a subject of care

classes.label: clinical care plan
classes.description: care plan addressing clinical issues

classes.label: clinical issue
classes.description: health issue that is a clinical matter

classes.label: clinical matter
classes.description: health matter concerned with the body or mind

classes.label: clinical need
classes.description: health need related to the body or mind

classes.label: composition
classes.description: structure component containing information composed during one clinical encounter or documentation session, and committed within one health record

notes.text: A composition represents the set of record components composed (authored) during one clinical encounter or documentation session, and committed within one electronic health record.

classes.label: consent competence
classes.description: capability of the subject of care and/or the subject of care proxy to give or withhold informed consent

classes.label: considered condition
classes.description: potential health condition considered by a care actor on the basis of one or more observed conditions

notes.text: A request for care normally includes a health condition or symptom observed by the subject of care and also a question about what the reason for that symptom might be. It is the potential health condition in this question (the health condition behind the symptom) that is called a considered condition.

A referral within a clinical process is normally motivated by one or several observed conditions and/or professionally assessed conditions. However the referral also normally includes a question that the care investigation is supposed to get an answer to. The question formulated as a potential condition is a considered condition.

A considered condition remains considered until the associated observed conditions are changed or completed. Care investigation or care treatment can result in new observations that can verify or not verify the considered condition. When a considered condition is verified it becomes an observed condition or professionally assessed condition that may be a working diagnosis. If a considered condition is discounted it is transformed into an excluded condition.
N/A

examples.text: S       Diagnostic hypothesis, any candidate in a differential diagnosis set.

classes.label: continuity facilitator mandate
classes.description: care mandate assigning the right and obligation to monitor and coordinate the delivery of care to one subject of care including the right to retrieve relevant information and obligation to document all activities based on this mandate

notes.text: Beyond solely assuming the function described above, a continuity facilitator may also assume the function of a lead and/or coordinator of care activities delivered to the subject of care
A continuity facilitation can be fulfilled only if the involved care actors have the information needed to perform their tasks in care activities
For continuity of care the continuity facilitator mandates for complete continuity of care processes are of special importance from the subject of care's perspective.

examples.text: A mandate assigned to a coordinating nurse, to a lead General Practitioner, key-worker, etc. to coordinate care

classes.label: continuity of care process
classes.description: care process, encompassing all care provider activities and other prescribed care activities, that addresses identified or specified health issues

notes.text: The continuity of care process can be regarded as the process to support continuity of care from the perspective of the subject of care.
In a continuity of care process a subject of care and care professionals interact in all types of care activities.
A continuity of care process comprises all kinds of care activities, including self-care activities as prescribed or recommended by care professionals.

classes.label: core care plan
classes.description: knowledge providing reusable content addressing one or more specified health issues

notes.text: A core care plan is usually based upon knowledge in clinical guidelines (including protocols).
Core care plans may be applied in care planning as a continuity of care process management method.
A core care plan may include advanced formulated schemes for recommended care activities.

classes.label: discharge report
classes.description: health report concerning a completed, mandated period of care

examples.text: Discharge letters

classes.label: discharge summary
classes.description: discharge report summarizing a mandated period of care

notes.text: A discharge summary may be provided to the subject of care.
One mandated period of care may be immediately followed by another; the next mandated care provider will then be a main recipient of the discharge summary.

Discharge summaries are often meant to be processed so as to categorize hospital stays according to a statistically designed classification (such as the Diagnosis-Related Groups (DRGs) or other systems), usually for funding or epidemiological purposes.

examples.text: 'Uniform Hospital Discharge Data Set' and 'Uniform Ambulatory Medical Care Minimum Data Set' in the USA
'Résumé de sortie standardisé' (RSS) or 'Résumé d'Unité Médicale' (RUM) in France
Nursing minimal data set (NMDS)

classes.label: encounter
classes.description: care activity period during which a contact occurs

notes.text: Since during a contact, more than one health issue may be addressed, it may relate to more than one care process and more than one episode of care.
In EN 13940-1:2007 the concept encounter was defined as 'contact in the course of which care activities are delivered to a subject of care in her or his presence'.

examples.text: Face to face contact with a GP, telephone contact, telemedicine contact.

classes.label: entry
classes.description: health record component that represents the data documenting one  observation or a series of related observations, or a  statement, an explanation of reasoning, a care intention, plan or action

classes.label: episode of care
classes.description: health related period during which care activities are performed to address one health issue as identified by one care professional

notes.text: An episode of care encompasses all care activity related to the same health issue.

An episode of care starts with the very first contact with a care provider for a health matter and it ends after the completion of all care activity periods related to the last contact with that care provider for the same health matter.

For practical reasons (e.g. the need to state start and end dates) and also because it relates specifically to a health issue defined by a given care professional, an episode of care does not necessarily coincide with an 'episode of illness' (or an episode of disease, or and episode of any other kind of health issue).
During a mandated period of care several health issues may be handled and as such be linked to several episodes of care. These episodes of care are said to be 'concurrent'.

examples.text: Period where a urinary tract infection is managed
Period where a cholecystectomy is performed

classes.label: episodes of care bundle
classes.description: group of episodes of care delineated by a health thread

notes.text: An episodes of care bundle starts with the very first contact with a care provider for a health issue considered in a health thread and ends after the completion of all care activities related to the last contact with any care provider for a health issue encompassed in the same health thread.

As different health threads may be considered that reconcile the perspectives of different care actors (e.g. a care team manager, or a health authority) or sets of care actors, there can exist different health threads according to the specific perspectives of those sets of care actors that justify building up such health threads. As a consequence there can exist as many episodes of care bundles as there are such health threads.
From the electronic health record point of view, an episodes of care bundle shows the overall care activity period elements related to those health matters that are linked by the same health thread.

examples.text: A group of episodes addressing diabetes mellitus with ocular and renal complications

classes.label: event
classes.description: situation considered to occur at a time point

classes.label: excluded condition
alsoKnownAs: discounted condition, non-verified condition, ruled out condition, ruled out considered condition
classes.description: considered condition that one or more care professionals have determined not to be consistent with the known observed conditions

classes.label: facility
classes.description: place, building or equipment used for a particular purpose

classes.label: folder
classes.description: structure component used for creating hierarchical structures within an electronic health record extract and with compositions as leaf nodes

classes.label: health approach
alsoKnownAs: care approach, goal-addressing episode of care
classes.description: episode of care during which the care activities performed address one specific care goal

notes.text: While the episode of care focuses the health issue addressed by the activities, the health approach focuses the desired outcome of these activities.

examples.text: During an episode of care social activities can include family therapy, medical treatment of subject of care abusing their partner and supporting talk therapy for the partner. A health approach can be restricted to talk therapy for the partner (with the goal to make them more calm).

During an episode of care a subject of care with diabetes mellitus is monitored and prescribed medication, self-control of habits of living. A health approach can mean that the blood glucose value shall be kept between 5 mmol/l and 8 mmol/l

classes.label: health certificate
alsoKnownAs: certificate related to a care matter
classes.description: official document issued by a care actor attesting the relevant health matters of a subject of care

examples.text: Birth certificate
Death certificate
Health certificate
Health insurance certificate
Vaccination passport

classes.label: health concern
classes.description: health record extract that includes all health record components associated with a health thread for a specific concern

notes.text: A concern is gathered information to support continuity of care for a subject of care. A health thread can include care activities, health conditions, care activity planning, activity management, evaluations and assessments. Thereby, all information needed for continuity of care for an individual subject of care can be covered by a health concern.

Health concerns can be constructed to support continuity of care for care processes, continuity of care processes and accumulations/associations of continuity of care processes. This means that concerns can relate to episodes of care, contacts, chronic disease management, multiple chronic disease management, etc.
Health concerns can be represented and labelled in electronic health records as extracts or components.
Health concern is closely related to the “concern tracking” based on HL7 standards.

examples.text: Concern tracker (HL7).

classes.label: health condition
classes.description: aspect of a health state

classes.label: health condition assessment
classes.description: care activity where an assessment related to health conditions of the subject of care is made

classes.label: health condition delay
classes.description: care activity delay caused by a health condition

examples.text: Surgery delayed because the subject of care is pregnant
Cervical examination delayed during menstruation
Vaccination delayed because the subject of care has a contraindicating infection

classes.label: health condition period
classes.description: health related period during which a health condition has been observed

notes.text: Health condition period refers only to the observation of the health condition, for example, the time interval during which a subject of care has observed a bleeding. The concept episode of care is referring to the care activities.

classes.label: health issue
classes.description: health condition motivating care

notes.text: A health issue can correspond to a health problem, a disease, an illness.

examples.text: Loss of weight, heart attack, drug addiction, injury, dermatitis, requirement for a health certificate, inability to prepare meals, suitability for immunisation

classes.label: health matter
classes.description: circumstance contributing to the health state of a person

notes.text: This can be a matter which calls for care intervention, e.g. a medical statement, and immunisation, a treatment but it may also be a matter which the person can manage alone e.g. by means of self-care activities or needs no management.

classes.label: health need
classes.description: health matter causing a deficit in the current health state compared to aspects of a desired future health state

notes.text: The desired future health state can be a health objective expressed as target conditions.
The health need can be identified and formulated by the subject of care or by any other care actor.
Health needs indicate and motivate care activities. They are the basic input to care needs assessments

classes.label: health problem
classes.description: health issue considered by a care actor to be a problem

notes.text: Health problems can be single observations but are usually more compound as a summary of several observations. Single observations are often criteria for the more compound health condition considered to be a health problem.

examples.text: S       Diabetes, stroke, heredity for breast cancer.

classes.label: health problem list
classes.description: health thread linking a set of health problems

classes.label: health record
classes.description: data repository regarding the health and care of a subject of care

notes.text: A health record may include, for example, medical records, dental records, social care records.

classes.label: health record component
classes.description: part of a health record that is identifiable for the purposes of referencing and revision

notes.text: This document defines one health record component specialization, the electronic record component. However, as the content of a health record is not limited to information in electronic format, the content of health record components may be in formats other than electronic.
A health record component may itself result from an aggregation of multiple health record components.

classes.label: health record extract
classes.description: part or all of a health record extracted for the purpose of communication

notes.text: This International Standard defines one health record extract specialization - the electronic record extract. However, as the content of a health record is not limited to information in electronic format, the content of health record extracts may be in formats other than electronic.

classes.label: health related period
classes.description: time interval related to the health or care of a subject of care

notes.text: A health related period may be specialized in relation to a number of situations – a specific continuity of care process, care professional, a specific department, a specific health circumstance, etc.

A health related period is delineated by a 'start date and time' and an 'end date and time'. While the statement of the start date is generally easy by definition, the identification of the end date may be subject to specific rules that have to be agreed upon locally.

examples.text: Period of a head ache, period of pneumonia, period of diabetes mellitus from first onset and often until death, period of receiving or delivering psychotherapy, period of social authority surveillance of suspected abuse in a family, period where a care provider is mandated to provide care for a subject of care's health issue, period of investigation and therapy of prostate cancer, period of delivering antibiotic therapy
Period of financial support

classes.label: health report
classes.description: health record extract conveying specifically focused care information in order to fulfil current information needs of the recipient

examples.text: Laboratory report
X-Ray report

classes.label: health state
classes.description: overall level of health of a subject of care

notes.text: Health is aspirational.
The underlying health state is present, even if not observed.  For example, the subject of care having a cancer before it gives symptoms.

classes.label: health summary
classes.description: electronic health record extract containing essential care information intended for specific uses

examples.text: Patient summary conforming to ISO 27269 - Health informatics — International patient summary
epSOS patient summary in the European Union
Clinical summary in the United States of America
Continuity care record  in the United States of America

classes.label: health thread
classes.description: defined association between health matters as determined by one or more care actors

notes.text: A health thread reconciles a range of health matters reflecting the variety of scopes of care actors, particularly of care providers.
A health thread may be established by a team (e.g. a coordination committee).
A health thread can be built step-by-step, by allowing each care professional to add their perspective into a common health thread.

Under the responsibility of a designated care actor, a health thread linking several health matters can describe an episodes of care bundle, for instance, a partial or comprehensive synthesis of care actor related episodes of care.
A collective decision (before, during or after the care interventions) may define a health thread and so the idea of the 'episode' accepted by all the care professionals involved.

Two health conditions are sometimes only be recognized as belonging to the same health thread late in the process of care. Conversely, two health conditions thought initially to belong to the same health thread may need to be separated later.
A health thread may be considered an aggregation of health matters and/or health threads.
N/A

examples.text: A low back pain, known for many years by the subject of care's GP, treated several times by the physiotherapist who labelled it a scoliosis and currently a candidate for a specific orthopaedic intervention.
Type 2 diabetes treated by a GP, a nurse, an endocrinologist and a vascular surgeon.
The health conditions included in a care process.

classes.label: indirect care activity period
classes.description: care activity period without the involvement of the subject of care

examples.text: The period where care activities are performed without the presence of the subject of care in order to decide whether a referral or a request for initial contact should be accepted or not
Time spent working on laboratory or tissue specimens taken from a subject of care
Period taken for the planning of care

classes.label: informed consent
classes.description: permission to perform care activities, voluntarily given by a subject of care with consent competence or by a subject of care proxy with consent competence after having been informed about the purpose and the possible results of the care activities

notes.text: A care mandate requires either informed consent or authorization by law.

classes.label: initial contact
classes.description: care contact starting a continuity of care process

classes.label: initial request for care
classes.description: first request for care concerning one or more specific health matters to be assessed by a care provider

classes.label: input health state
classes.description: health state at the start of a care process

notes.text: The output health state from one care process may be the input health state to a subsequent care process.

classes.label: integrated care plan
alsoKnownAs: multi-professional care plan, multi-disciplinary care plan
classes.description: care plan crossing organizational, professional and contextual borders

notes.text: Integrated care plans are the basis for planning to deliver continuity of care
Integrated care plans address physical, mental and social needs

classes.label: intended outcome
classes.description: desired achievement of a care process addressing health needs

notes.text: An intended outcome can be expressed as one or several target conditions to be reached within a specified date and time.

examples.text: To increase the survival of a subject of care with breast cancer in a breast cancer screening programme.
To reduce a subject of care risk of morbidity and mortality from tobacco-related diseases in a smoking cessation programme.
To decrease a subject of care risk of secondary effects to diabetes

classes.label: knowledge
classes.description: maintained, processed, and interpreted information

classes.label: mandate to export personal information
classes.description: care mandate implying the right to communicate health record extracts

examples.text: A request to a doctor to write a letter to a medical specialist stating the reasons of a referral
A request to a General Practitioner by a subject of care for the transfer of his or her health record to another General Practitioner, when that subject of care moves to another part of the country.

classes.label: mandated period of care
classes.description: set of care activity periods where a care provider is mandated to perform the care activities required to address specific health needs

notes.text: The mandated period of care is focused upon the framework of a care commitment of the provider as well as the mandate from the subject of care, which means that the roles and responsibilities of both the interacting parts are respected.

Whenever the care provider considered in a mandated period of care is a care organization, this mandated period of care encompasses all care activity periods with care professionals who have a role in that care organization.
A mandated period of care may be part of another mandated period of care.
The relevant information describing and produced during the care activities performed as a part of a mandated period of care is recorded in one or more professional health records.

examples.text: A hospital stay
A series of radiotherapy sessions at an outpatient clinic

classes.label: medical device
classes.description:
instrument, apparatus, implement, machine, appliance, implant, reagent for in vitro use, software, material or other similar or related article, intended by the manufacturer to be used, alone or in combination, for human beings, for one or more of the specific medical purpose(s) of
— diagnosis, prevention, monitoring, treatment or alleviation of disease,
— diagnosis, monitoring, treatment, alleviation of or compensation for an injury,
— investigation, replacement, modification, or support of the anatomy or of a physiological process,
— supporting or sustaining life,
— control of conception,
— disinfection of medical devices,
— providing information by means of in vitro examination of specimens derived from the human body, and which does not achieve its primary intended action by pharmacological, immunological or metabolic means, in or on the human body, but which can be assisted in its intended function by such means

notes.text:
Products which can be considered to be medical devices in some jurisdictions but not in others include:
— disinfection substances,
— aids for persons with disabilities,
— devices incorporating animal and/or human tissues,
— devices for in-vitro fertilization or assisted reproductive technologies.

classes.label: medicinal product
classes.description: pharmaceutical product or combination of pharmaceutical products that may be administered to human beings for treating or preventing disease, with the aim/purpose of making a medical diagnosis or to restore, correct or modify physiological functions

notes.text: A medicinal product may contain in the packaging one or more manufactured items and one or more pharmaceutical products. In certain regions, a medicinal product may also be defined as any substance or combination of substances which may be used to make a medical diagnosis.

The provisions in this document apply to proprietary medicinal products for human use intended to be placed on the market and to be industrially manufactured medicinal products, the marketing of which has been authorised by a medicines regulatory agency. However, the provisions do not apply to: 
i) medicinal products prepared according to prescription (e.g. prepared in a pharmacy from a prescription intended for a specific patient), 
ii) medicinal products prepared in accordance with an official formula (e.g. prepared in a pharmacy in accordance with the instructions in a pharmacopoeia and intended to be given direct to the patient by the pharmacy), 
iii) medicinal products intended for research and development trials and to 
iv) intermediate products intended for subsequent processing by an authorised manufacturer.

classes.label: mental object
classes.description: thoughts, ideas, memories and concepts that exist within an individual’s mind

notes.text: Mental objects are dependent on an intentional agent.

classes.label: needed care activity
classes.description: care activity bundle which includes those care activities assessed as needed to address specified health need

notes.text: Needed care activities are managed in a care plan

A needed care activity is the outcome of care needs assessments performed by care professionals. Needed care activities can be identified by any mandated care professional performing care needs assessment for a subject of care

classes.label: non-ratified care information
classes.description: care information the relevance of which has not been assessed and explicitly stated as valid by a care professional

notes.text: Care information that has been received and is proposed for incorporation in a professional health record may not yet have been reviewed for validity or context by the care professional, and as such its accuracy may remain arguable. In this respect, clinical ratification differs both from attesting information for the needs of an audit trail, or from committing information in the perspective of sending out messages containing care information, outlined in EN ISO 13606-1:2008.

Regardless of the technical process meant practically, care information cannot be 'inserted' into a professional health record unless ratified by a care professional. Until a decision is made regarding their insertion or rejection, they are held in a secure temporary storage.  An exception to this would be when a care professional would directly import care information from a sharable data repository into the local health record they hold. In the case where this import resulted from a request to the care actor in the custody of whom the sharable data repository is placed, that care information would then need validation.

classes.label: objection
classes.description: subject of care desire that specific care activities are not performed

classes.label: observed condition
classes.description: health condition observed by a care actor

notes.text: Care professionals and subjects of care are examples of care actors that can perceive the observed aspect of a health state.
An observed condition is a health issue and as such is a representation of one or more aspects of the health state.

examples.text: A blood pressure, a swelling in the abdomen, tachycardia, body weight, lung infiltration on x-ray, a haemoglobin value, pale skin.

classes.label: other carer
classes.description: person being a care third party

examples.text: A relative (family member), a neighbour.

classes.label: output health state
classes.description: health state at the end of a care process

classes.label: personal health record
classes.description: health record held and maintained by the subject of care or a subject of care proxy

classes.label: point of care
classes.description: location where direct care activities are performed

notes.text: Location refers to the geographical location of the subject of care, not the body area of the subject of care that the treatment is applied to.
Activities referred are not limited to care provider activities; self care activities are included

examples.text: Surgery room, ward, ambulance, roadside, home of the subject of care, school, etc.

classes.label: potential health condition
classes.description: possible future or current health condition described by a care actor

notes.text: A potential health condition is not yet observed, but represents an imagined, possible observation of aspects of a current or future health state.
A potential health condition can only be fully supported through the use of one of its specializations.

classes.label: prescribed self-care
classes.description: self-care prescribed by a care professional

classes.label: prescribed self-care period
classes.description: care activity period where prescribed self-care activity is performed

classes.label: prescribed third party activity
classes.description: care third party activity prescribed by a care professional

classes.label: prescriber
classes.description: role performed by a care professional when recommending or authorising a care activity

classes.label: professional health record
classes.description: health record held under the responsibility of one care provider and maintained by care professionals

notes.text: The responsible care provider may allow the subject of care to access and/or offer contributions to the professional health record.

examples.text: Health records held at their surgery or at a health centre by a GP, by a medical specialist, by a nurse, in a hospital department at a patient's bedside, by a care team in an integrated clinical network, by a dentist.

classes.label: professionally assessed condition
classes.description: observed condition assessed by a care professional

classes.label: prognostic condition
classes.description: potential health condition representing the expected course of a health state as assessed by care professionals

classes.label: protocol
classes.description: customized care guideline

notes.text: A protocol is more precise than a care guideline.
Protocols are often presented in a formal manner with respect to the expected behaviours and roles of care actors.

examples.text: A protocol for a heart failure in an emergency department, a protocol for treatment and follow-up of urinary infections in children in a health centre.

classes.label: proxy mandate
classes.description: care mandate to take decisions on behalf of a subject of care

notes.text: A proxy mandate may be explicit, such as a power of attorney, or implicit, such as parental responsibility.
A proxy mandate may be granted through informed consent of the subject of care or acquired by legal means, such as parenthood.

classes.label: reason for request for care
classes.description: motive for a request for care

notes.text: There are needs for both direct (care investigating and care treatment) and indirect (care assessments, care planning, care evaluation, etc.) care activities.

classes.label: record template
classes.description: template for recording a care record

notes.text: In HL7 FHIR, care record templates are known as questionnaires; the completed health record is a questionnaire response.

ISO/IEC TS 19763-13:2016 Information technology — Metamodel framework for interoperability (MFI) Part 13: Metamodel for form design registration provides a metamodel for form design registration suitable for representing care record templates; care record templates are known as form designs in both ISO 19763-13 and ISO ISO/CD 21526 Health informatics — Metadata repository requirements (MetaRep).
In openEHR, care record templates are known as templates
A care record template acts as a data constraint on a health record used in specific care provision use cases

classes.label: referral
classes.description: request for care where a care professional asks a care provider to make a care commitment for a care period mandate

notes.text: An accepted referral transfers the continuity responsibility for the health issues specified in the referral.

examples.text: A referral from an orthopaedic surgeon to a rehabilitation service.

classes.label: request for care
classes.description: request for care provider activities

notes.text: A request for care may be expressed either by the subject of care or on their behalf.
A care provider may accept, refer or decline a request for care.
A request for care could result in a care assessment concluding that no other care activity (as care investigations and/or care treatments) should be performed.

examples.text: An emergency call to a General Practitioner for a home visit at night.
A request for an appointment at a cardiology outpatient department.
A request for the intervention of a community nurse.

classes.label: request for service
classes.description: request for care where a care professional asks a care provider to perform one or more care provider activities

notes.text: The responsibility for the requested care provider activities is held by the performer but they will be performed under the care period mandate of the requester.
A care provider may accept or decline a request for service

examples.text: Request for a care assessment, an operation, a wheelchair, etc.
Request for a second opinion where the responsibility remains with the requesting party

classes.label: request mandate
classes.description: care mandate implying the right and obligation to request care activities

notes.text: A request for care is usually made by a subject of care him/herself, but there are circumstances where the subject of care is not in the position of making a demand for care. In that case, it has to be made on their behalf by another person such as decision making representative with power of attorney.

Within certain jurisdictions a generic demand mandate may be explicitly or implicitly assigned to certain categories of citizens on the basis of their specific roles. It may also be directly or indirectly made necessary by law that such generic mandates are explicitly established.
Every care professional with authority (mandate) to manage health matters of a subject of care is entitled to request services and has a request mandate.

examples.text: A subject of care requesting care from a General Practitioner.
Care for a child requested by a parent.
A passer-by may, in some jurisdictions, be obliged to seek care for any endangered person, for example unconscious after a road accident.

classes.label: resource delay
classes.description: care activity delay caused by resource constraints

examples.text: Care activity scheduled later than clinically indicated to allow resources to be deployed on other subjects of care (a waiting list)
Care activity postponed while necessary financial resources are identified

classes.label: resultant condition
classes.description: health condition representing an output health state

notes.text: A resultant condition can represent the output health state after a single care activity, a bundle of care investigations and/or care treatments in a care process.  It can also represent the outcome after a complete continuity of care process.

examples.text: Care process result, care activity result

classes.label: risk condition
classes.description: health condition representing a potential undesired future health state

notes.text: While a risk is defined as the combination of a probability of an event and its consequences, the risk condition deals only with the consequences.
A risk condition can be a result of a current vulnerability

classes.label: role
classes.description: set of competencies or performances that are associated with a task

classes.label: self-care
classes.description: care activity performed by the subject of care

examples.text: Self injection of insulin, self-measurement of blood pressure, or of glycaemia, yoga, taking a bath, meditation, mindfulness

classes.label: self-care performer
classes.description: role played by a subject of care when performing self-care

classes.label: sharable data repository
classes.description: data repository containing health record extracts, accessible for duly authorized care actors independent of their organizational affiliation and placed under the custody of a care actor

notes.text: A sharable data repository has to be placed under the custody of a care actor in order to assure and maintain its consistency.

classes.label: social care
classes.description: care activity supporting a subject of care to live in their social environment

classes.label: social care activity mandate
classes.description: care mandate to provide needed care activities concerned with the social environment of a subject of care

classes.label: social care plan
classes.description: care plan addressing social issues

classes.label: social care report
classes.description: social record extract conveying specifically focused social care information in order to fulfil current information needs of the recipient

examples.text: report on the home visit done by the social worker
disability level measured adopting a specific scale method

classes.label: social environment
classes.description: immediate physical and social setting in which a subject of care lives and interacts

classes.label: social issue
classes.description: health issue that is a social matter

classes.label: social matter
classes.description: health matter concerning how a subject of care lives in their social environment

classes.label: social need
classes.description: health need related to social determinants of health

examples.text: loss of job
gambling addiction
loss of personal autonomy
physical or mental disability

classes.label: social record extract
classes.description: part or all of a social record extracted for the purpose of communication

classes.label: stative
classes.description: a state or condition

notes.text: A stative expresses a state or condition rather than an activity or event

classes.label: subject of care
classes.description: person seeking to receive, receiving, or having received care

notes.text: A fœtus may be considered as a subject of care when receiving care.
N/A

examples.text: A treated patient
A client of a physiotherapist
Each particular member of a target population for screening
Each particular member of a group of diabetic people attending a session of medical education
A person seeking health advice

classes.label: subject of care desire
classes.description: desire expressed by the subject of care or the subject of care proxy regarding the performance of certain care activities

examples.text: No blood transfusion, cultural or religious preference, do not resuscitate, allow natural death
Preference for a monetary subsidy instead of a direct social service to support a disabled person
To be supported/treated by same gender social care/care professional
To specify the place of care (home, professional office, ...) where to receive a service (psychological support)
To receive a financial/economic support instead of a social service
To prefer home services instead of being moved in a retirement home

classes.label: subject of care preference delay
classes.description: care activity delay by the preference of the subject of care

examples.text: Surgery delayed to enable the subject of care to undertake seasonal work
Investigation delayed to support the subject of care’s scheduling choice

classes.label: subject of care proxy
classes.description: care actor exercising a proxy mandate

classes.label: summarized care information repository
classes.description: data repository containing summarized information for care coordination and the continuity of care

classes.label: target condition
classes.description: potential health condition representing either health objectives or care goals

notes.text: Assessment of needs for care activities includes identification of health objectives and/or care goals. These inform decisions about relevant activities to create or update the care plan.

examples.text: The target condition for a worker that arrived at the Emergency Room with a broken arm is to be fully functional for work in the shortest time period.

The target condition of a newly diagnosed diabetic adolescent boy is maintenance of his HbA1c at less than 48 mmol/mol. (HbA1c is a lab test that shows the average level of blood sugar (glucose) over the previous 3 months; it shows how well diabetes is being controlled).

classes.label: third party care performer
classes.description: role performed by a care third party

classes.label: unintended event
classes.description: phenomenon that is not part of the normal course of a process

notes.text: An unintended event can be either expected or unexpected.

Activities in a process are deliberate and have a purpose. In an ideal situation purposes are always fulfilled. If an activity in whatever other process has an impact on the process currently analysed, the effect of this activity is perceived by the current process as an unintended event. Then the course of the process may deviate from the expected one. Such an exception from the desired course might prove negative or positive in comparison to the desired process outcome.

examples.text: Surgical complication (anatomy and tissue reacts in an unexpected manner)
electrical failure
contamination in a medicinal product
hardware failure
spontaneous recovery when the patient is awaiting therapy

classes.label: vulnerability
classes.description: health condition that is an exposure to the risk of harm

notes.text: In the perspective of health care and wellbeing the term vulnerable condition is often used to label frail citizens and/or socio-economic and education conditions (e.g. housing, job, disability) that motivate certain interventions in social care, generally addressing a target population (e.g. older persons, children, families, homeless, refugees).
The potential harm is a risk condition

examples.text: Released from prison
Drug addiction
Loss of employment
Lives up a steep flight of stairs leading to increased risk of falls
Immunosuppressed leading to risk of infection

classes.label: working diagnosis
classes.description: considered condition that one or more care professionals have determined to be the most consistent with observed conditions

notes.text: A working diagnosis is used as a label for the considered condition that one or more care professionals assess as the most probable health condition and that could be concluded after further observations. The basis for such assessments is the already observed conditions.
Different care professionals may make different interpretations and assessments of the observed conditions and thereby come to different conclusions and different working diagnoses.

A working diagnosis is often identified in the continuity of care process as a summary after the planned care investigation are completed. A working diagnosis in this stage is often called a diagnosis. An excluded condition could correspondingly be called a negation of a working diagnosis.
